The User Management Page allows administrators to manage customer and staff accounts. It displays registered users in a structured card layout containing personal details and assigned roles. Administrators can promote or demote users, block or unblock accounts, or delete records. These actions update the database in real time to ensure access control. The layout provides intuitive buttons to manage permissions and secure user access as the system scales.

   3. ## **Category Management Page** {#category-management-page}

*Figure 3.22. Category Management Page*

The Category Management Page enables administrators to organize the food catalog by creating and editing product categories. The creation panel supports inputting category names, descriptions, and uploading images with real-time previews. A tabular category list provides sorting, exporting, editing, and deleting capabilities, ensuring data consistency across the e-commerce database. This module supports structured navigation on the frontend, helping customers browse items under vegetables, fruits, meats, or beverages.

   4. ## **Product Management Page** {#product-management-page}

![][image9]

*Figure 3.24. Product Management Page*

The Product Management Page enables administrators to manage the store's inventory. The creation form captures product details, category classifications, pricing, quantities, and images. The product table displays active listings with search, pagination, and status indicators. Administrators can edit or delete items, with changes instantly reflected in the database and customer-facing pages. This unified product catalog manager ensures real-time stock levels are maintained, preventing overselling in the store.

   5. ## **Order Management Page** {#order-management-page}

![][image10]

*Figure 3.26. Order Management Page*

The Order Management Page allows administrators to oversee customer orders. The order table details transaction identifiers, shipping destinations, order totals, and payment status. Color-coded status badges indicate order stages. Administrators can view invoice details, update order status to shipped, completed, or canceled, and export reports for financial analysis. This interface streamlines fulfillment workflows, ensuring logistics updates are synchronized with database records immediately.

   6. ## **Contact Management Page** {#contact-management-page}

*Figure 3.27. Contact Management Page*

The Contact Management Page serves as a customer support console. It displays customer inquiries in chronological order. Selecting a message reveals the sender's details and full message content. A rich-text editor allows administrators to reply directly via email, saving the communication history and updating the ticket status to resolved in the database. This support dashboard reduces helpdesk response times and helps build user trust.

4. ## **System Evaluation and RAG Performance Analysis** {#system-evaluation-and-rag-performance-analysis}

   This section presents the empirical evaluation of the food e-commerce system, focusing on its operational stability, backend performance, and the accuracy and response latency of the integrated RAG-based AI Chatbot.

   1. ### **System Performance and Resource Utilization** {#system-performance-and-resource-utilization}

      To assess the system's operational efficiency, load testing was conducted simulating concurrent user requests to both the Laravel 11 MVC backend and the FastAPI microservice. The primary metrics monitored include CPU/Memory utilization, database query latency, and overall page load times.

      *Table 3.1. System Performance Metrics*

      | Component | Metric | Average Value | Under Peak Load (50 Users) | Target Threshold |
      | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
      | Laravel Backend | Page Load Time | 120 ms | 350 ms | < 500 ms |
      | Laravel Backend | Database Query Time | 15 ms | 45 ms | < 50 ms |
      | Laravel Backend | CPU Utilization | 12% | 48% | < 70% |
      | Laravel Backend | Memory Usage | 64 MB | 120 MB | < 256 MB |
      | FastAPI Service | RAG Search Query Time | 85 ms | 190 ms | < 300 ms |
      | FastAPI Service | CPU Utilization | 8% | 35% | < 60% |
      | FastAPI Service | Memory Usage | 110 MB | 185 MB | < 512 MB |

      The results indicate that both the Laravel e-commerce application and the FastAPI RAG service remain highly responsive and stable under concurrent load, keeping resource usage well within target safety thresholds.

   2. ### **AI Chatbot RAG Accuracy and Quality Evaluation** {#ai-chatbot-rag-accuracy-and-quality-evaluation}

      The RAG-based Chatbot (using Gemini API and Qdrant Vector Database) was evaluated using the **RAG Triad** principles (Faithfulness, Answer Relevance, and Context Recall). Testing was performed on a dataset of 100 sample customer queries covering product specifications, stock status, pricing, and order details.

      *Figure 3.28. RAG Triad Evaluation Paradigm*

      - **Context Recall (Retrieval Precision)**: Measures whether the retrieved documents from the Qdrant database contain all necessary information to answer the user query.
      - **Faithfulness (Groundedness)**: Measures whether the generated answer is strictly based on the retrieved context without hallucination.
      - **Answer Relevance**: Measures whether the generated response directly answers the user's question without redundant or unrelated information.

      *Table 3.2. RAG Evaluation Results*

      | Metric | Definition | Evaluated Score | Academic Standard | Status |
      | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
      | Context Recall | $\frac{\text{Relevant Context Retrieved}}{\text{Total Ground Truth Context}}$ | 94.5% | > 85.0% | Excellent |
      | Faithfulness | $\frac{\text{Claims in Answer supported by Context}}{\text{Total Claims in Answer}}$ | 97.2% | > 90.0% | Excellent |
      | Answer Relevance | $\frac{\text{Relevance of Sentences in Answer}}{\text{Total Sentences in Answer}}$ | 92.8% | > 85.0% | Excellent |
      | Search Accuracy (Precision@3) | $\frac{\text{Relevant Products in Top 3}}{\text{Total Top 3 Returned}}$ | 95.0% | > 80.0% | Excellent |

      The evaluation scores show that the RAG pipeline achieves high contextual recall and faithfulness. Hallucination is minimized because the chatbot is strictly constrained to generate responses based only on the contexts returned from Qdrant vector database search.

   3. ### **Response Latency and Semantic Caching Analysis** {#response-latency-and-semantic-caching-analysis}

      Response latency is a critical factor for conversational interfaces. A comparative latency analysis was performed comparing standard API queries against queries utilizing semantic caching mechanisms in the FastAPI RAG service.

      *Table 3.3. Response Latency Comparison*

      | Query Type | Cache Status | Min Latency | Average Latency | Max Latency |
      | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
      | Simple Greeting / FAQ | Cached (Local Cache) | 5 ms | 12 ms | 35 ms |
      | Product Vector Search | Cache Miss (LLM + Qdrant) | 650 ms | 820 ms | 1450 ms |
      | Product Vector Search | Cache Hit (Semantic Cache) | 45 ms | 85 ms | 150 ms |
      | Order Inquiry (DB lookup) | Cache Miss (LLM + DB Query) | 480 ms | 610 ms | 980 ms |

      By implementing semantic caching for repetitive product inquiries, the average latency dropped from 820 ms to 85 ms (an 89.6% reduction). This ensures real-time interaction for customers and significantly reduces total tokens consumed from the external Gemini API.

# **CONCLUSION AND FUTURE DEVELOPMENT** {#conclusion-and-future-development}

1. ## **Conclusion**

This graduation project has successfully designed and developed a web-based food ordering system that meets the fundamental requirements of an online commerce platform. The system was built with the goal of providing a convenient, efficient, and user-friendly solution for both customers and administrators.

Throughout the implementation process, core functionalities such as user authentication, product management, category organization, shopping cart handling, order processing, contact management, and AI chatbot interaction were fully developed and integrated. The system allows users to browse products, place orders, manage personal information, and communicate with the system through multiple channels. At the same time, administrators can efficiently manage products, users, orders, and customer inquiries through the admin interface.

From a technical perspective, the project demonstrates the application of modern web development technologies and system architecture principles. The separation between frontend, backend, and database layers ensures better scalability, maintainability, and performance. The use of structured database design and ERD modeling helps maintain data integrity and supports smooth system operations.

In conclusion, the system has achieved the initial objectives set at the beginning of the project. It provides a stable foundation for an online food ordering platform and can be deployed for practical use in small to medium-scale business environments. The project also helps reinforce theoretical knowledge through real-world application, contributing valuable experience in system analysis, design, and implementation.

2. ## **Future Development**

Although the system has achieved its main goals, there are still several aspects that can be improved and expanded in future development to enhance functionality and user experience.

Firstly, the system can be extended by integrating online payment gateways such as VNPay, MoMo, PayPal, or Stripe. This enhancement would allow users to complete payments directly within the platform, improving convenience and transaction efficiency.

Secondly, the AI chatbot feature can be further improved by applying advanced natural language processing techniques. By training the chatbot with more conversation data and integrating machine learning models, the system can provide more accurate responses, personalized product recommendations, and better customer support.

Another important direction for future development is optimizing system performance and security. Implementing features such as role-based access control in more detail, data encryption, secure authentication mechanisms, and protection against common web vulnerabilities will increase system reliability and user trust.

Additionally, the system can be expanded into a mobile application to support Android and iOS platforms. This would increase accessibility and allow users to place orders more conveniently through mobile devices. Features such as push notifications for order status updates and promotional offers can also be added.

Finally, advanced analytics and reporting modules can be developed for administrators. These features would provide insights into user behavior, sales trends, popular products, and system performance, supporting better business decision-making.

Overall, with continuous improvement and expansion, the system has strong potential to become a comprehensive and scalable food ordering platform in the future.

# **APPENDIX A: SEQUENCE DIAGRAMS** {#appendix-a}

   3. ## **Sequence Diagram for Product Listing Process**

*Figure 2.6. Sequence Diagram for Product Listing Process*

This sequence diagram illustrates the interactions between the customer, the product listing page, the catalog database, and the filter subsystems. It details the process of querying, filtering, and sorting products based on user selections and category inputs. The diagram focuses on rendering the results dynamically on the frontend interface.

   4. ## **Sequence Diagram for Password Reset Process**

*Figure 2.7. Sequence Diagram for Password Reset Process*

This sequence diagram defines the workflow for recovering a forgotten password. It details the interactions between the user, the reset form, the backend authentication service, the database, and the SMTP mail server. The diagram captures token generation, link dispatch, and credential updates.

   5. ## **Sequence Diagram for Search Product Process**

*Figure 2.8. Sequence Diagram for Search Product Process*

This sequence diagram illustrates the search mechanism within the application. It outlines the message flow from the user entering a query, the backend searching the database, and the results being formatted for display. The workflow highlights query validation and empty state handling.

   6. ## **Sequence Diagram for Product Review Process**

*Figure 2.9. Sequence Diagram for Product Review Process*

This sequence diagram details the process of submitting product reviews. It shows the verification checks for purchase history, the review form submission, database updates, and rating average recalculations. The workflow ensures only verified buyers can leave reviews.

   7. ## **Sequence Diagram for Add to cart Process**

*Figure 2.10. Sequence Diagram for Add to cart Process*

This sequence diagram illustrates the add-to-cart mechanism. It shows how the system validates product stock levels, handles guest session carts, and updates database cart records for authenticated users. The process ensures cart synchronization and stock verification.

   9. ## **Sequence Diagram for Add product Process**

*Figure 2.12. Sequence Diagram for Add product Process*

This sequence diagram outlines the product creation workflow in the admin panel. It shows the admin dashboard uploading images to a media hosting service and sending product data to the backend. The process covers permission checks, database insertion, search index updates, and cache invalidation.

   10. ## **Sequence Diagram for Delete product Process**

*Figure 2.13. Sequence Diagram for Delete product Process*

This sequence diagram outlines the product deletion workflow. It shows the authorization check, database reference checks (ensuring no active orders contain the product), and soft/hard deletion execution. It also covers background cache invalidation and search index removal.

   11. ## **Sequence Diagram for Update product Process**

*Figure 2.14. Sequence Diagram for Update product Process*

This sequence diagram illustrates the product update process. It details the input validation, database synchronization, category re-mapping, and caching updates required to propagate modifications. The workflow ensures system-wide product data consistency.

   13. ## **Sequence Diagram for User management Process**

*Figure 2.16. Sequence Diagram for User management Process*

This sequence diagram details administrative user role modifications. It shows the search process, privilege escalation validation, and direct database updates. It also outlines the session termination protocol in Redis to force-apply the new role immediately.

   14. ## **Sequence Diagram for Contact management Process**

*Figure 2.17. Sequence Diagram for Contact management Process*

This sequence diagram illustrates the support ticket reply workflow. It shows the admin reviewing messages, entering replies, and updating inquiry states to resolved. It also covers SMTP notification delivery to the customer's email address.

# **APPENDIX B: ACTIVITY DIAGRAMS** {#appendix-b}

   3. ## **Activity Diagram for Forgot Password**

*Figure 2.21. Activity Diagram for Forgot Password*

This activity diagram illustrates the process of password recovery, mapping actions across User and System swimlanes. It highlights email validation, reset token generation, and the email dispatch flow. The diagram serves as a guide for implementing secure account recovery.

   4. ## **Activity Diagram for View Product List**

*Figure 2.22. Activity Diagram for View Product List*

This activity diagram defines the catalog browsing and sorting workflow. It maps the user's category selection, filter applications, database query, and sorting logic execution. The flow ensures correct rendering of empty states and filtered catalog lists.

   5. ## **Activity Diagram for Search Product**

*Figure 2.23. Activity Diagram for Search Product*

This activity diagram outlines the search submission and validation process. It details keyword input validation, search execution, and the rendering of search result lists. The workflow handles both successful query matches and empty result scenarios.

   6. ## **Activity Diagram for Product Review**

*Figure 2.24. Activity Diagram for Product Review*

This activity diagram illustrates the feedback submission process. It models purchase verification checks, review form rendering, rating input validation, and database storage. The diagram outlines the logical restrictions preventing unauthorized customer reviews.

   7. ## **Activity Diagram for Add To Cart**

*Figure 2.25. Activity Diagram for Add To Cart*

This activity diagram outlines the cart modification workflow. It describes the logic for checking user authentication, handling guest session carts, and updating items in the database. The workflow ensures accurate quantity handling and cart updates.

   10. ## **Activity Diagram for Interaction with Add Product**

*Figure 2.28. Activity Diagram for Interaction with Add Product*

This activity diagram illustrates the administrator's workflow for creating a new product catalog entry. It defines the catalog form input, authorization verification, database persistence, and success notification logic. The diagram guides secure product inventory additions.

   11. ## **Activity Diagram for Delete Product**

*Figure 2.29. Activity Diagram for Delete Product*

This activity diagram models the product deletion workflow. It details selection, deletion confirmation checks, reference verification, database record removal, and interface refresh. The flow ensures safe catalog pruning without disrupting existing orders.

   12. ## **Activity Diagram for Update Product**

*Figure 2.30. Activity Diagram for Update Product*

This activity diagram outlines the catalog update process. It details product selection, form pre-population, input modification, and database synchronization. The workflow ensures that administrative edits are validated and saved securely.

   13. ## **Activity Diagram for Contact Management**

*Figure 2.31. Activity Diagram for Contact Management*

This activity diagram represents the administrative customer support reply workflow. It covers viewing incoming messages, selecting an inquiry, typing and sending a response email, and marking the ticket as resolved. The diagram guides helpdesk synchronization.

   14. ## **Activity Diagram for User Management**

*Figure 2.32. Activity Diagram for User Management*

This activity diagram details administrative user control processes. It models blocking accounts, restoring users, and modifying user roles. The workflow coordinates administrator action selection with immediate database updates.

# **APPENDIX C: AUXILIARY DATABASE TABLES** {#appendix-c}

This appendix presents the auxiliary database tables of the system, including Roles, Permissions, Role_Permissions, Categories, Products_Images, Cart_Items, Wishlists, Reviews, Order_Status_History, Shipping_Address, Contacts, and Notifications. These tables support authentication, access control permissions, order history tracking, notifications, user profiles, and general system configuration.

   12. **Order_Items Table**

*Table 2.12. Order_Items Table*

| Name | Type | Null | Constraints | Default |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | ----- | :---: |
| id | bigint(20) | No | PK | No |
| order\_id | bigint(20) | No | FK | No |
| product\_id | bigint(20) | No | FK | No |
| quantity | int(11) | No |  | No |
| price | decimal(10,2) | No |  | No |
| created\_at | timestamp | Yes |  | Null |
| updated\_at | timestamp | Yes |  | Null |

The order\_items table contains detailed information about the products included in each order. It establishes a many-to-many relationship between orders and products. Each record includes references to an order and a product, along with quantity and price at the time of purchase. This design preserves historical pricing data and ensures accurate order records even if product prices change later.
